summary: This installment of Cathy O'Neil's advice column 'Ask Aunt Pythia' covers four reader questions while also promoting a satirical column called 'Dear Miss Disruption' that skewers tech industry bootstrap mythology. The questions range from practical (pursuing a statistics degree as a career changer) to interpersonal (accepting money from a generous friend) to the bizarre (incest revelations in a relationship). O'Neil's responses blend practical wisdom with characteristic directness, and she notably highlights Miss Disruption's sharp parody of tech industry privilege, which mocks the 'learn to code' narrative by exposing the hidden advantages behind supposed self-made success stories.
key_points: The 'learn to code' bootstrap narrative often obscures the privilege (family wealth, elite education, seed capital) that actually enables tech success
When pursuing career changes into quantitative fields, formal credentials may matter more for admissions than actual learning outcomes
Money between friends can be a healthy exchange when given freely and received graciously, matching each person's natural abundance
Advice columnists must always consider whether questions are fabricated, especially those with sensational elements
money_quotes: Other than my affluent Orange County family, my Stanford bachelor's degree, and the $10 million that my uncle invested as seed capital for my innovative advice column start-up, I have nothing but my ability to code.
It might be more convincing to the admissions people to see an official set of courses in math and programming with official grades than for you to tell them you learned it on your own.
Some of them are just cash cows.
She wanted to give you something in return that was her kind of offhand bounty that she thought might help you with your life.
Go buy ingredients for some more waffles.
That's what I got for this question.
It's probably even more likely given the incest theme. But whatever, I'm almost out of questions.
key_insight: The tech industry's 'pull yourself up by your bootstraps through coding' mythology conveniently ignores the family wealth, elite credentials, and seed capital that actually enable most success stories.
